content:
Member-only story

5 min read
Just now

--

Perfectionism isn’t a quest for software engineering excellence. It is a sophisticated psychological defense mechanism to avoid shipping and facing the market.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

If you are not a Medium Member, you can read full article here: [Link]
We have all met that senior developer who lives in a self-imposed purgatory of continuous refactoring. They have spent three weeks re-architecting a domain layer for a feature that hasn’t even seen a single production request. They argue passionately about strict DDD boundaries, micro-optimization of database queries, and whether a concrete implementation deserves an interface when there is only one logical consumer in the entire codebase. From a psychological standpoint, we like to label this as a profound commitment to craftsmanship. But let’s be entirely honest with ourselves over this coffee: it is nothing more than high-IQ procrastination designed to keep your work safe from the cold, indifferent gaze of a real user.
The clinical reality is that perfectionism in high-performing technical profiles is rarely a deficit of confidence. It is a symptom of defensive narcissism. You have constructed a mental monument to your own…
